Some of you may know, and some of you send me something. I always like getting them, so keep sending them. I collect, I really like dumb criminal news stories. There used to be a syndicated television show called America's Dumbest Criminals, and it was a cheaply made show, but it was great, and my market was on Saturdays, and they had four or five stories, and always said really stupid crooks. Usually, if I travel, I get the small community newspapers if I can, because the main city may not have it, but the small community papers usually have the dumb crook stories, and all of that. So I collect them. I probably have a thousand of them by now, but I always like new ones. And some of the fairly recent favorites, well one's an old one, but it's so good, but one is, and this I think happens all the time, but very recently this guy got himself arrested because the bank he held up, he wrote the note on the back of his own deposit slip, and that's what he gave the teller. We actually have a guy at the track who has a few horses who was a bank manager, and he put on a mask and robbed his own bank, which his logic about this was he knew it intimately, so that would be the good one to rob. Of course, the guy's like six foot four with a fairly distinctive voice, and the staff all knew him, you know, not real smart. There's a great, the original founder of Entrepreneur Magazine, the original founder, not the current people, but way back. He would occasionally get himself in money trouble, and so he's telling me this one morning at breakfast at a seminar where we're both speaking, and I didn't know it, but he had a lot of tattoos, which sometimes, so he'd done a little time. So he decided, he was in trouble, he was in Los Angeles, it was all over the news in LA, he just didn't register with me, but he decided to get himself out of trouble by robbing a bunch of banks in one day, and the plan he concocted is a pretty smart guy, actually, so he mapped himself out of route, and he figured out a way to rent a motor home with fake ID, and he went down to where you pick up people with no green cards to be day laborers, to one of those parking lots, and he picked up a bunch of guys, he deliberately picked the ones that didn't speak English real well, and he had watches for all of them that could be set to the time that they were supposed to be back out where he dropped them, and he gave them all notes and envelopes and bags, and conveyed to them that they were supposed to take that up to the teller, and so his strategy was to drop them all and then go back and pick them up at the appointed times, and if they were there, he'd pick them up, if they weren't, he'd just drive on by, if there was a bunch of cop cars there, and he just wouldn't pick them up, right? And so, excuse me, so this worked, and I don't remember the number now, but it was seven or eight or nine banks, and the same day they all got robbed, and he picked everybody up, drove them back to the lot, dumped them off, cops finally found them, a bunch of guys don't speak English, right? All they can do is watch, big truck, they don't know. But he said, you know, he said, it's such a, it just, I just couldn't bear the thought of having gone to all the trouble to figure all that out and do it only once. So, three weeks goes by and nobody bothers him, so he does it again, and not only does he do it again, he does the exact same route, because why reinvent the wheel, right? This time, of course, they were waiting for him, and he did some time. There's two guys, this is true, Colorado, they robbed this convenience store, out in the middle of nowhere, and they jump in their car and they take off like a bat out of hell, because they know the cops are coming, it's in this small town up in the Colorado Mountains, and it's not their normal turf, and so in very short order, they're hopelessly lost, driving 900 miles an hour up and down the mountain through the pine forest, they have no idea where they are, or how to get back to wherever it is they came from, and they're actually worried about running out of gas, but up ahead through the fog, good news, there's a convenience store. Yes, see, I shouldn't need to finish this one. So, these buffoons go back to the same convenience store that they robbed to stop and get directions, and they both go in, because one's going to get directions, the other one's going to get beer, because they got money, and of course, the guy's waiting for him with a double-barreled shotgun, and some Darwinian principles apply. Two more, there's a guy, he robs a store or a bank, I forget which, in town, the cops chase him to his home, and he hides in the deep freeze, the big square like you might have in your garage or your basement. So, his is in the kitchen, actually, so he takes all the frozen food out of the deep freeze, piles it in the sink, and then he hides in the deep freeze. Now, small town cops may not be rocket scientists, but as they come in to search the house, they notice this giant, you know, horde of melting food, and brilliantly put two and two together, but they very slowly and meticulously search the entire house before arriving at the half-frozen guy in the deep freeze, but here's the best one of all. This guy, very small town, I think it was an auto parts store, some kind of store, he robs, takes all the merchandise home, tools and stuff, and is going to sell them on eBay. So, he takes all the stolen goods home to his trailer, no surprise, and of course he's got to put them on eBay so he has to photograph them. So, he puts each thing on the floor and photographs it, right, barefoot, and his feet are in the picture. Now, in every one of the pictures that he puts up on eBay, now remember, it's a very small town, this moron has very distinctive tattooed feet, they are completely covered with tattoos, everybody in town knows his feet. So, his stuff is up on eBay for about an hour before the police arrive at his door and take him away. Now, I have a theory about crime in general, I think that there's a lot of people who shouldn't go to jail for what they go to jail for, but that we should have a federal penitentiary for the criminally stupid. See, this is the place that say Martha Stewart should have gone, okay, no, you shouldn't go for obstruction of justice, but for jeopardizing the whole empire for $15,000 on the stock trade, you should go do a year at the prison for the criminally stupid. Simpson, for going and trying to steal back his memorabilia when you sort of know you're an unpopular character who might not do well with law enforcement or in a courtroom, I'm not so sure the guy should go to jail for going and getting his stuff, even at gunpoint, but he should go for being criminally stupid. And exercise every day should be to come out in the yard and take your place and smash your head against the wall for 15 minutes and go back into your cell. So that when you got out, maybe you would be smarter, you know, that's my theory. So, here's what I detect in a lot of business people. Criminal stupidity about devoting their time, energy, money and resources to selling to people with less money rather than selling to people with more money. So think about all the criminals who rob convenience stores. Can you be any dumber? There's very little money to get. They all have surveillance cameras. They all have safes that they drop the money into every hour, so there's not a lot of money in the cash register. And a whole lot of those guys have guns. So there's very little to get. There's a reasonably good chance of getting shot. And you're immortalized on film. And there's a big sign on the window that says security camera, just in case you weren't aware of this fact. Which if you're a convenience store customer, you like, no, and pretty much everybody that robs convenience stores are convenience store customers. They could go rob mansions. There's more junk laying around in the foyer that's worth more than the money they're going to get at the convenience store and they can rob them when nobody's home. And very few people who live in mansions have double-barreled shotguns. I mean, it happens, but not often. They tend to have little yappy dogs like our million-dollar dog that'll just bark at them a little while and then befriend them. But they don't have double-barreled shotguns, right? So why not rob mansions instead of convenience stores? And the question is, are you doing the same thing in your business? So you spend a lot of time trying to rob convenience stores when just a short... The main reason they don't, by the way, is because they'd have to drive out of where they live 10, 15 miles out to where the mansions are, and that's just too much. You know, they want to rob something that they can walk to or take the bus to. Driving 15 miles is too much to ask of a career criminal. And business people are largely the same. It's just too much to ask to figure out how to go this short distance away, not necessarily geographically, from where I am in order to deal with mansions and the people who live in them instead of the equivalent of convenience stores and the people who own that. So most business owners have no strategy about this whatsoever. Their customer acquisition strategy is whoever we can get. That's their customer acquisition strategy, is whoever we can get. And the problem with that is, is first of all, it costs just as much to get a poor customer as it does to get... Joan Rivers, who I did a bunch of work for, you know, Joan's advice to women always was, you know, you can fall in and out of love with a rich guy just as easily as you can a poor guy. Pick the former rather than the latter. Somebody said, amen. I knew we weren't going to get all the way to the end of the day. It's impossible, right? You were going to screw with me. So the same thing is true with customers. See, it's not proportionately difficult to go get a rich customer than a poor customer. But pretty much everybody's strategy is any customer we can get. But if you understand, as we talked about in an earlier module, the customer's the asset. So you're making an investment in an asset. So don't you want the most valuable asset that you can acquire? So marketing to more affluent customers is a strategy that really every business owner, whether you're a consumer or B2B, doesn't make any difference. Needs to give some serious thought to. Why don't more do it? Well, most business owners are handicapped in marketing to the affluent, not just by skills, by practical skills, by not knowing, but also by attitudes. And both have to be given a little consideration. So practical needs, what do you need to know? Well, you need to know where they are. Where are the more affluent buyers for my particular type of business? And how do I reach them? You need to know what these more affluent customers, clients, patients want. So how can I be appealing to them? You need to know their language so you can talk to them in a way that is acceptable to them and does not mark you as inappropriate for them. And so this is sort of like geographic, but now it's tribal. So if somebody who's spent their whole time in a small town, Mayberry RFD in South Carolina, goes to New York or goes to Boston to do business, they immediately have difficulty. Because they're immediately marked because of their language as a rub. If the person who's lived in Boston their whole life goes to Mayberry RFD in South Carolina, they have difficulty because they're immediately marked as an elite Easterner. Well, the same thing is true here, even though it's not about geography, it's still tribal. So there's language to be learned so you can talk to them effectively. And there's the design of a marketing system that they will respond to. Attitudinally, the obstacles may be greater. One is you have to really believe that there's an ocean of money slopping around out there all the time. And again, at the moment that we're recording this, there's even more said about that negatively than at other times. There is this perception, this real attempt to sell you on the idea that nobody has any money and nobody's buying anything. And our population is completely made up of people hanging on by their fingernails who certainly can't be spending anything. And if you're not careful, you sort of begin to believe that too. And it really is just you're looking in the wrong places and listening to the wrong folks. And we'll talk about that a little bit. But in order to do this, in order to market more effectively, to get more fluent customers, you first have to grasp that there's plenty of them to get. And that they are spending plenty of money, even while other people may not be spending or may be spending less. Secondly, you have to understand them. And so if you are not one of them and you are not of this tribe, you will tend to think their behavior odd or weird or even not believe the behavior exists. And so you need to understand them. And then you have to get over your own personal reluctance to sell to them. And so we're going to talk about both the practical and the attitude. Let's start with is there any money? Well, not fairly recently. The most recent Batman movie had a $150 million opening. So people spend $150 million going to the movies. Understand that's just what we spent on tickets. That's not what we spent on popcorn, soda pop, gummy bears, etc. Stopping at restaurants before they went, after they went. That's just tickets. $150 million in a three day period of time to go see a movie. Filling the restaurants around the theaters. And by the way, everybody had to use gas to get there and get back. Now if you listen to the media, you understand everybody's got their car up on blocks. Because nobody can afford gas. But somehow they all managed to get to the movie theaters. They also managed to get to the malls to line up and wait in line to buy iPhones. Now are there stores in those malls not doing well? Yes. At the same time that a thousand people were standing in line waiting to buy an iPhone. So there's a thousand people there and they're spending money on something. So the guy looking at the line with his story that's not doing well, say he says, economy's bad, nobody's spending any money even while he's looking at them. No, you just don't have anything exciting enough for them to all lined up and spend money on. It's not that they're not spending $4 gallon gas on most recent holiday weekend. The holiday driving was reduced by 1%. 1%. Everybody went filled up and everybody went 1% reduction. Here's what's really true about fluctuations in the economy. Money is short only in a few select segments. And in other segments, it's just fine. There's geographic variation, there's type of product service variation. But overall, the same amount of spending occurs. Now investment dollars park. Spending dollars actually don't. Maybe they should, different topic of conversation, but they don't. They just move around differently, but they keep moving. And things, one business that's hurting begets another business that is either created or wasn't doing well that is now doing well. I just, I picked it up yesterday. This is my local business journal. And here's the headline. In the wake of foreclosure mess, the business of maintaining abandoned homes booms. And so this is an article about a big company that is growing by leaps and bounds, that comes in, clears all the trash out, cleans up the abandoned house, and makes sure it stays in good condition until it gets old. So they can't hire people fast enough. They can't accommodate the demand in Cleveland for their service. Every foreclosure to them is, let's have a round of applause. Here's good news. Oh boy, there's more. Goodie, goodie, goodie. Let's hire more people. And now if you read the article deeper, now they're making so much money, they don't really need the money, so they're starting to buy because they're seeing them all. So they're starting to buy the best bargains of the homes that they're being hired to take care of. And when all the dust settles, they're going to own a ton of good real estate that they bought at Nichols and Dimes on the Dollar with the money they were making by running the business. SUV sales, not so good. Commuter motorcycle sales, real good. Harley's rushing to the market. Everybody else already had one, but Harley kind of stuck their nose up at it, but they're rushing out the tricycle because there's a big demand for commuter bikes. People who wouldn't normally ride a motorcycle will ride one of these things to go back and forth to work. The other thing you should know about this is that the classes just divided. So you hear all the time that the American middle class has disappeared. And that's somewhat true, but it's not like the alien mothership came and sucked them all up. And they're gone. They went somewhere, right? They didn't physically disappear. Their demographics changed. So where did they go? One-third moved down, two-thirds moved up. There's a new class called middle class millionaires. And even with the recent reversals in real estate value, it's still the fastest growing demographic in America. With the same money, it just got redistributed. And there's actually more affluent customers than there were before. Guess what's going to happen over the next decade? A lot more affluent customers than there were before. Can't help but be. About to have the largest wealth transfer generationally ever. Your parents, my parents, they're leaving us. They got trillions captive in houses we aren't going to want. In low-yield investment products, all that money's going to be liberated and slap around and wash around again. And change hands four, five, six times. So there's plenty of money. And a lot of it gets spent as dumb money. And we just have no idea if you don't get. So here's just a couple of examples. This is... Is there a photographer in the room? I know there's a videographer because he was talking to me about it. Is there anybody in here that's actually in photography business? Huh? Huh? Yeah. Huh? Well, here's this guy's day-weight to do weddings. It's $10,000 an hour, 12-hour minimum. If you want, you can get something scheduled for late 09. Late 09. You know how many photographers are running around trying to get $100 an hour? $200 an hour? $300 an hour? The guy's got a waiting list until late 09. I'll show you some other examples later. If you don't pay attention, you can get something scheduled for late 09. I'll show you some other examples later. If you don't pay attention, you have no idea. There's a level of money being spent, and it's easier to get that than it is to get the low dollar. So why work? Why should we work at attracting more fluent customers? Well, one, a more secure business. In troubled times, they are least and last affected. In good times, they spend freest. In troubled times, they're least and last affected. So the person who dines at Olive Garden, I dated this gal for two years. Right after high school. And her father hated me. And in retrospect, I don't blame him. This guy was a middle manager, executive at Goodyear, Tire. He lived in Akron, Ohio. And he was a gray person. Meaning hair, skin color, clothes, car, personality, all gray. You could set your watch by when the back of his head appeared in the front window of his house every day. This is how predictable he was. So from work to home to TV tray, drink, Wall Street Journal, back of head, pop up on the window. Exactly the same place, exactly the same time. He took his wife out twice a year on her birthday and on their anniversary. And I swear to God, on the birthday he took her to Bob's Big Boy every year. And now this is not like that Putz John Edwards, we go to Wendy's on her anniversary, nonsense. This is for real. This guy took her every year to Bob's Big Boy. And for their wedding anniversary, a step up, he took her to the Brown Derby, which is kind of like a mid-range, like an outback level steak place. Well, I like ruin this in like three months. Because I mean, when I dated, I dated. So this is now, his deal is like blown apart. Because the daughter's going to more places in a week than he's taken the wife to in 25 years, better places, you know, with regaling the stories. And so he hated me, which I understand. But see this guy, the first thing he'll do in an economy like this is cut one of those two out. And he will be immediately affected, if not in reality, emotionally. Oh, we got to cut back, because that's the way he thinks already anyway. The person who stays at the Ritz and goes to Morton's, affected slower and less affected. So which customer do you want? Which builds a more secure business? The first businesses to get in trouble are the ones middle to low, all the way at the bottom, okay? Because people buy necessities from them, people keep buying on necessities. All the way at the top, okay? Because we have this kind of customer in between, not so good. More secure business. Second, less competition. It's real important. Everybody thinks it's harder to get these customers. It's not because there's fewer people with the Cajones to go after them. There's fewer people trying to get the affluent customer, and even fewer trying to get the ultra affluent customer, than there are trying to get Bonn, Parkwater, who go to Bob's Big Boy on their anniversary. A lot of people trying to get that. The competition is massive. There's a lot less competition here. The further up the demographic ladder you go, less competition. There's a Walgreens and a CVS opposite each other on every corner. Who are they trying to get? And they're both selling, what's a lipstick and a Walgreens go for? I don't even know. I mean, what, a couple bucks still? I don't even know. Anybody know? Eight bucks. Eight bucks, okay. All right. But there's not a Sephora store on every corner. There's a limited number of Sephora stores. And then there's a whole other level of cosmetics. The $800 lipstick. All right. Oh, yeah, there's $800 lipstick. The guy just went, oh, yeah. Hey, let me tell you something. There's probably $8,000. There's probably $8,000 lipsticks. I just don't happen to know about it, but I know about the $800 lipstick. You know? That exists. And by the way, somebody somewhere is probably putting it on a pig. They're less price sensitive. These customers are less price sensitive. So what kind of customer do you want? You want a customer who does not make their decisions based on price. Now, only 10% of the people make their decisions based on price all the time. They have no choices. All right. But there's about 25% of the population that never makes a decision based on price. It's not one of their criteria. They buy what they want to buy, what they're convinced they should buy, what they feel they need. They typically try and buy the best of what it is that they buy. And price is not a consideration. At the ultra-fluent level, they shop in places where there are no price tags. I vividly remember the first time I saw this. Yes, I thought this was the most amazing thing. I bumbled into a store on Rodeo Drive in Beverly Hills. I'm shooting an infomercial on LA. And I bumble in this store because I saw a jacket that I liked. And there's no price tag. So, okay, they screwed up. They left it off this one, right? Check this one. No price tag. Finally asked the clerk, I get the boat answer. You know, sir, if you have to inquire about the price, this is probably not the appropriate store for you. So I bought the thing and found out what it cost when you ran the cart. I never did that again, by the way. But there are customers, a big chunk of customers, 25% of the population. Don't decide by price. So would you rather have a price-sensitive customer or a non-price-sensitive customer? Each customer is obviously more valuable, which means you can invest more to acquire them and invest more to keep them. So where is the money? Who has it? Well, the old middle class, of course, is gone. The people with 40-year careers working at the factory, that's all over with. We know it's all over with. But there's this whole new middle class, the middle class millionaire that has occurred and they have higher discretionary spending capability than ever. There are all sorts of segments within the affluent. So there are a whole segment now of independent female buyers and investors who almost did not buy much and did not invest much before. They are in cosmetic surgery and cosmetic dentistry. They are ideal clients. The sweet spot for them is six months after the divorce and they got the house. That's the ideal prospect. Because they're getting back in the game and they're going to go get something done. Most of them are going to get a lot done and they have the ability to pay for it. There are now almost as many female investors as there are male investors. They didn't use to be the case. So the quote-quote independent woman consumer that has recently, as a decade ago, did not exist for big-ticket items and investments and financial services. They're now a very real category. The boomers have an enormous amount of discretionary spending power and a different attitude about spending than any previous group reaching the same age. Every previous group reaching the same age had a stop-spending mentality. They downsized, they kept what they had, and they began to save every penny to leave to their children. We don't have that attitude. A lot of us are on my plan. Spend it all, die broke. Let them go get their own just like I did. Here's a copy of Think and Grow Rich. Have at it. All right? I'm buying another horse. Sorry, but that's the deal. I want the last check to bounce. And I want to spend every penny. They're spending like there's no tomorrow. There's got two houses, three houses. Taking vacations, buying, buying, buying, buying, buying. And they're about to have more money than they have now. There's other segments, too. We don't have time to talk about it. The Marketing to the Fluent book. I talk about a bunch of them. So your tasks are escape all kinds of poverty thinking about yourself and about others, about the marketplace. Now, here's the biggie. You need to find out for yourself, for your business, who has strong urgent motivating need or passionate desire for what you offer and has ample money to buy it and makes those decisions without significant concern over price. There is somebody, there is a group of people for whatever it is that you do. Wedding photography? Death photography. Probably got a better term for it, don't we? The end of life ceremonial, whatever it is. Wedding photography, death photography. Probably sell a package. There's nothing wrong with prepay, you know? There's a group of people for whatever it is that you do who fit this description. They have urgent motivating need or passionate desire. They have ample money to buy and they make their decisions without significant concern over price. And that's who you want to find. And if you must do some makeovers to your business, in order to suit them, go ahead. What are they spending it on? Well, they're spending a ton of money on personal passions. Collecting, travel, motorcycles, exotic cars, gourmet foods. Exotic car business is booming. The massive fluent article somebody just sent me, every car maker is rushing more convertibles to the marketplace. Why? Because boomers. It's a midlife crisis car. So everybody's pushing commercials out there and it's the most stable segment of the market. Who's buying a car a fluent 45 to 55 year olds. So everybody's coming to the market with convertibles. Kids, they're spending a ton of money on kids. Unbelievable amounts of money. I watch my daughter and our three, our grandkids, they're in 500 activities. Our activity was go play in the yard. And the second instruction was don't come back till dinner. That was the organized activity. Now you need a full time driver and a bus to get in a schedule and a blackberry to organize where they're going. It's amazing what they're spending. Michelle Obama literally said in one interview, I'm sure Barack has no idea that we spend $10,000 a year just on our children's activities. She said this was a problem everybody has, by the way. Everybody has. And maybe they do. Pets, the new kids. Pet business, what's people spending on pets? Through the roof. We spend conservatively on the million dollar dog compared to some of what I see. I mean, at least the spa we put her in, we don't order in food from Morton's for her. But customers can do that. They have a takeout menu and they can pre-order meals from Morton's and they bring in steak dinners for the dog. I mean, please. But they're doing it for the house. You've all seen the grand kitchens. They're the four people that have grand kitchens who don't cook. They also watch the food channel, which is like soft-core porn for people who like food. It's apparently what this is. It's like the Playboy channel, but everybody's got their clothes on and stuff smeared all over them. It's as near as I can tell. Because everybody I know who watches it doesn't cook. So what do you watch it if, oh, I just like it? Well, it's the same as people watching porn. They don't have sex either, but they like it. Luxury goods. Luxury goods. So here's a catalog. Here's a catalog. This happens to be a very successful company. I can't even pronounce it. I don't know. Is that Gorsuch? I don't know what it is. Anybody a customer of theirs? Gorsuch? Is that what it is? No, no, no, no. That's a political statement and politics are off limits here. You should be ashamed of yourself. Okay. Now this is actually a subculture catalog, by the way. This is like for people who have ski chalets. It's really what this catalog is for. So here's a... I don't know if you can see this. See this hat here? This is a little knit cap with some kind of little fur thing on top of it. Anybody want to guess? Ah, not to see Susan. You're definitely hanging out with this crowd, aren't you? $129. So you would consider this a bargain. See? See? See if we were playing prices right, see? She'd be high. So by the way, she's single, but in case anybody was looking at her, she just said a little cloth hat with a little fur thing on top. She'd cost you $250. You might want to buy a little fur thing. She just said a little cloth hat with a little fur thing on top. She'd cost you $250. You might want to rethink that little plan. Okay? Here's a nice looking sweater. Zips up and down. Got little black stripes on it. $399. But wait, it gets really good here. So here's a guy's sweater. As near as I can tell, this is a blue pullover with three buttons on it. Anybody want to guess? Susan? $1,148. $1,148. Here's a...no, the guy doesn't come with it. No. No. Who asked that? No. I'll bet you he's less than the cost of the sweater actually. Okay. Okay. There's a dress. Not much, but there's a dress. And there's a sweater. Oh, and there's a belt. Okay? The sweater...yeah, that's cashmere. The sweater's $1,998. The dress is $998. This is the best part. The belt is $698. $698. $698 for the belt. Okay? The belt. Here's a blouse. It's got some kind of animal print over it. Okay? $1,598. And the black jeans are $778. By the way, it's a catalog you don't want to take home, guys. Now, here's a v-neck sweater. Okay? It's cashmere, two-ply cashmere, and it's got some little beading around the neck. Okay? It is $998. Here is a v-neck sweater. It's two-ply...this is a different catalog. Two-ply cashmere. It doesn't have the little cubic zirconia beading around the neck, but still, it's $38. Okay? $998. $38. Fundamentally, the same sweater. That's an extremely important lesson about price and who is spending what on what. The same spread exists in everything, whether you see it, are aware of it, believe it, or not. There's a $38 thing. There's a $900 thing. Somewhere, there's one more expensive than the $900 thing. And there's a customer for every one of them. There's customers for every price point. I'm sure you've heard the sale of the glasses that Governor Sarah Palin is wearing are through the rough. The optical stores can't get enough of them. They had a big run on them right after her convention speech. And women are all getting their hair done like that, too. So we're pretty sure we're going to have a little army of them walking around. Which, if they all go learn how to kill and dress moose, see, that'd be really interesting, but that's like half. But the frames are $337, by the way, is what they are for those frames, which much has been made out of. So there's a ton of people who have rushed out to buy $337 frames. I'll bet you a bunch of them have never paid $337 for frames before. But now, their desire level was raised to the point that the price became irrelevant. But $337 for a pair of glass frames, to some people, that sounds like a lot. But you can spend $3,000. There's a price for everything. And every category, get this, for everything, for every category, there is somebody spending freely on it at premium price levels. You, because of your own experience, may not be aware of it. You, because of your own biases and who you sell to now, may not even believe it. But it exists. There is always a way to spend more for something. There's always somebody who has figured out how to charge more and has found out that there are customers for it, for everything, different price levels, different customers. So, let's talk a little bit about gaining confidence in marketing and selling to the affluent, because a lot of this has to do with how you feel about it before you can actually do it. So there's a thing called marketing and sales are reluctance. People are reluctant to go target these kinds of customers and effectively sell to them. Why? First, they exaggerate the superiority of the affluent. They think that those people are somehow superior to them because they have more money than them. I have often said, if you took poor people and let them hang around with millionaires for a week, there would be a lot less poor people because they would discover the millionaires aren't any smarter than they are. In some cases, they're dumber than they are. The millionaires don't have better work ethic than they do. In many cases, they got poorer work ethic than the poor people do. The only difference between the millionaire and the poor person is the size of his bank account. And as soon as a bunch of poor people figured that out, you'd have less poor people. I know a lot of rich people. And some of them haven't got enough sense to come in out of the ring. You wonder how they just get around every day. But they got a ton of money. See, it's not about that. So there is no inherent superiority to them, but it's a big reason why people don't go try and sell to them. Because they have them above them here. Just because they got more money, not the case. There's a belief that the affluent client won't accept advice from someone less affluent than they are. Well, my doctor makes less per hour than I do. But I want his advice. My car mechanic makes a lot less money per hour than I do. But I damn sure want his advice because I know nothing. I have no earthly idea. I know key turn start. That's it. Beyond that, I don't know. My brother, who's poor, knows a lot about that, by the way. So he rides my car in five minutes and he can tell me, you need this, you need that. This is why that's due. I don't know any of that. And by the way, I don't want to know. But I want a mechanic who knows and I don't evaluate the mechanic based on his financial position in relationship to my financial position. I evaluate the mechanic based on his capability at making my car run. In almost every category, realtor, do I care whether they make as much money as I do, more money than I do, less money than I do? No, I do not. It's not how I'm going to pick a realtor. Even a category where maybe you should think about this. I've been through three money managers from my pension staff. I think the best one is probably a guy who makes the least amount of money. But he's really, really smart about two or three of those things. He really isn't even a very good business guy. But he's really, really, really smart about two or three of those things. So no, it's not about do you make as much money as they do. The people buying out of this catalog have no earthly idea who Gorsuch is and whether or not he made any money with his business last year. It doesn't even cross their mind when they pay 2,000 bucks for a sweater. That's not how they evaluate that. But people let this get in their way. There's a restaurant in Phoenix. It's in downtown Phoenix. It's called a Matador. And it's kind of a rat hole Mexican place. In fact, I wouldn't go there without an escort. So I used to go occasionally for breakfast with a guy who worked for us, Joe Salcino, who was of that neighborhood. And so Joe would take us there. I would not have gone without Joe because I would have worried about getting back out. And the place is like a dump. And it attracts all sorts of rich people who go down. The food's great, by the way. Fabulous. The top of your head sweats, which is how you know you're eating good Mexican food. You know, just sweat here, you gotta sweat up here. And so, I mean, it's authentic, authentic, authentic, authentic. So authentic, you're afraid to go there without an escort. So I used to tell Joe, there's a business here of escorting rich white guys to go down there and eat who won't go there without an escort. But I mean, the Hoy Palloy are down there in this dump with mismatched chairs and for mica floor. And it is actually kind of a trendy thing to go do. There's nothing to do with how much money the restaurant owner makes. Inability to see a way to connect their product service and business to the affluence interests. Yeah. If you don't understand them, you can't figure out how what you do could connect to their particular needs, desires, interests and passions. And how you can tweak it so that it will justify not only their interest, but their premium price patronage. So you gotta really understand them. And fourth, this is a biggie. Resistance to the change in the work involved. This is a biggie. It stops a lot of people because they don't want to work. Here's what I would say to you. All of the trends are going to continue in the direction they are going. So what little is left of the lower middle class, those folks that have manufacturing, industrial jobs who have them for 40 years and make 50, 60, 70 thousand dollars a year before it's over with and can buy a lot of things. The ones that are gone aren't coming back and the rest of them are leaving. That part of our society is all going to be gone. All of it. Gone, gone, gone, gone, gone, gone, gone. None of these trends are going to change. And all of it is going to divide one-third, two-thirds. Two-thirds are going to move up. One-thirds are going to move down. All of the complaining about the wealth being concentrated in fewer and fewer hands, the wealth is going to be concentrated in fewer and fewer hands. Right now it's 55 percent of the discretionary power is in 20 percent of the households. So by the way, you can decide whether to talk to the one in five, the four in five, or the five in five and take your chances. Me, I'd like to talk to the one in five where all the money is. That's just me. But see, that's not going to change. And all the Gorsuch's, all of them, they can't change it. Demographics are destiny. You can't change it. So none of that is going to change. It's just going to get more so. So in a not too distant future, it's going to be 60 percent of the discretionary spending in 17 percent of the households. That's where we're going. And they can screw with it all they want. Won't make any difference. Because if you don't change behavior and you don't change demographics, you can't change money. You can take it away from this one and you can give it to this one. And this one comes around here and gets it back. Because it's about behavior and it's about demographics. So all of, so you can choose to be on the right side of this. Early mentors said, there's always going to be a cash register. You can't eliminate the cash register. You can just decide which side you stand on. That's what you could do. So in this case, you can decide where you're going to be in relationship to all these trends. That's the only thing you can do. And the longer you wait to get your act together about getting and keeping more fluent customers, the further ahead others are going to be of you in doing so. But you're not going to change the trends. So how can we become more comfortable and confident in selling to the affluent? One, exposure. You got to get out more. You got to go where they are. You're never going to see what they behave like at the Four Seasons and the Rents if you stay at the Holiday Inn. You'll never know. You got to at least go over there, hang out in the lobby. You're never going to have an idea how boat owners behave. At least go to a boat show. At least go to a boat show. Go to the Jackson Barrett Exotic Car Auction. You want to see money move? Holy cow. Go to an Exotic Car Auction and watch money move. We just had a horse sale. My trainer picked out three to bid on. Now here's a frame of reference. We're in the middle of a recession. The state of Ohio, horse racing is not in good shape for a variety of reasons I won't bore you with. But it's one of the states that is severely troubled with that industry. Purse money which we compete for is down for the third year in a row. So here's the horse auction. So the one yesterday when I drove last night it came in and said did we get the horse? My trainer laughed and he said well they called me, the guy that was bidding for us. He said I told him he could go as high as 12,000 bucks and he said you'll probably get him for eight. You know what the horse sold for? $46,000. And every horse sold. Every horse sold. I can't even explain it to you. It makes no sense. But if you want to see money move, go to a horse auction. Go to a cattle auction. And by all means go to a classic car auction. You will be astounded. I called the first new car I had that I bought with my own money was a 1974 American Motors Javelin. For those of you that don't have a frame of reference is what they renamed Rambler. And the Javelin was a sporty car not to be confused with a sports car. But it was a real cool looking car. And so I decided a couple years ago I'd like to have one. Well it turns out they were really short supply because they weren't a very good car and none of them made it. Probably but I want one. So I'm haunting the car auction cattle auction. Finally there's one coming up at an auction. Now this is an American Motors Javelin. I paid $4,200 in change for it new. I call to find out what the reserves going to be. $40,000. They had at this auction and this is a minor auction. There's at least five or ten every weekend. This is a minor auction out in the middle of Nebraska somewhere. You've got to get a mule to get to. 800 cars up for auction. And I said how many of them aren't good? Oh she said none of them. We'd probably sell 70, 80% of them for above the reserves. There's no point in you you can bid long distance and stuff but there's no point in bidding if you don't want to spend $50,000 to $60,000 for that car. There's no point in it. A javelin. A javelin. $40,000. I want it but I don't want it $40,000 worth. I couldn't bear to do it. I could do it. I just... But there's going to be 600 cars sold that weekend at that auction alone. Vegas is off. You hear how Vegas is off? Trump sold out Tower One and sped up construction on Tower Two. High end is holding up. So you've got to get out more. You've got to go look at it. You've got to experience it so you really internally believe it. You've got to see it. Then you've got to understand it. You've got to understand it. So now you've got to make a study of these people. What makes them tick? Why do they buy? Why does Jay Leno want to have a garage with over 100 exotic cars in it? We know he can but the more salient question is why? Say, why? How many can you drive? How many can you even go look at? How many do you need? Why? Why do you want that? Because that, if you understand that, you can get money from Jay Leno. And there's surprisingly a lot of Jay Leno's. So you want to become a student of these people. What do they read? Where do they go? What do they do? What do they say to each other? How do they think? Idea three, this is maybe the most practical for you, is to study comparables. So comparables are who else is successfully selling to your targeted consumers or to the targeted affluent consumers you wish to sell to? They're probably selling non-competing products and services. But with comparable prices, comparable sales methods, comparable appeals, comparable media, there's something similar to what you do or want to do. Find the comparables. So I had a client some years ago who had exclusive rights to this whiz bang coffee maker thing that the prince or king of Bavaria uses in the castle. It's a huge crystal gold thing and it pours the water from here to here and back to here and steam comes out and it makes noise and it grinds the coffee and it kicks out little, little tiny little cups of them and it's a big production. And these things for years were only sold to real high-end restaurants and they'd have them because it's a show, you know, you wheel it over the table. So she decides she wants to sell it to homeowners, to consumers. This is a number of years ago and this is like an $8,000 coffee maker. And I said, not everybody's going to want an $8,000 coffee maker. So, you know, we got to figure out who's going to want an $8,000 coffee maker. So what do we got to find? We got to find somebody selling something equally stupid. Forgiver take $8,000. That's what we got to find. And then all we got to do is find out who they're selling to and how they're selling and we can copy it. It turns out, by the way, not a lot of comparables in that category. But there's a fair amount of comparables selling equally stupid stuff for $8,000 to banks and corporations to put in their boardrooms. Turned out there's a lot of that. We saw a lot of coffee makers to banks and corporations to put in their boardrooms. So comparables, go find comparables and then play prospect. And this is a real asset test, by the way, for you in all your marketing. I ask it of clients all the time. Okay, so you sell X and you sell it to this constituency. How many comparables have you gone and played prospect with? All the way to the point of not signing on the dotted line. None. Free research. Can't get cheaper than free. Free research. There's nobody you want to sell to that somebody else isn't already selling to. There's no price you want to sell at that somebody isn't already selling at that price. There's no method of selling you want to use that somebody isn't already using that method. Find them, play prospect. You'll learn a lot. You'll learn what to do. You'll learn what not to do. What works, what doesn't work. Study the best, study the worst, study everybody you can study. So if I was going to get into time share business, for example, I would go play prospect and let Disney try and sell me a time share. Who's going to do it better than Disney? I'd go to Fairfield Properties someplace because they've been in a business the longest. And I'd play prospect. I'd go to 8, 10, 12 of these places and play prospect. If I needed a little old lady, I'd rent me a little old lady, take a little old lady with me. If we needed to look super rich, I'd buy something out of here, dress her up. And I'd take her with me. But I'd play prospect. See, people don't even do that. So we talk about the work, you got to find out. And it's not that the information is not available, that it's hidden. It's not. So factors that control affluent consumers buying choices. This is readily available to you. It's in chapter 10 of the affluent book. There's the genera key factors that apply to everybody, right? So people buy because of these five reasons. Fear, pride, love, guilt, greed. That's why people pretty much do everything. The more of them you use, the better. They're really just nuanced, for affluent. But some things to consider, one, surprisingly, is insecurity. The more affluent, the more worried they tend to be about people taking it from them, about them losing it. See, poor people aren't really worried about the Dow yesterday. Poor today, poor tomorrow, they're a havers. AIG, ABC, NBC, what do we care? It takes a rich guy to be worried about that. Oh my God. So nobody's really sweating it unless they have a lot of money in the stock market. Insecurity, fear of being found fakers. There's a lot of affluent people who know the secret, that they are no smarter. They are no better. They are no faster. They are no quicker than the poor people. They just don't want anybody else to know. And so they spend money in certain ways to reinforce image and self-image. Desperate desire not to commit a faux pas. Don't want to be the one in the room using the wrong fork. And by the way, a whole lot of them don't know which fork. They watch him. They all wait. Nobody wants to be the first one. Five forks. Never saw five forks before. By the way, there's a rule about that. The more forks there's going to be on a table, the worse the food and the smaller the portions. So if there's going to be three or more forks, always take some crackers. Today, not passe. They don't want to be the last kid on the block. They're not conducive with their self-image. Feeding emotional emptiness, giving themselves gold stars, buying things no one else can have. Somebody said after all, what's the point of being a wrench? If I can't have stuff, nobody else can have. That's sort of the point. Here's the upscale differentiation. Here's what happens. You sell to the affluent, has recognition and reinforcement. They buy largely to reinforce where they already are. That's why they tend to buy the best option of all the options because they're affluent. That's what they're supposed to buy. The mass affluent, they buy for aspiration and achievement. So they buy ahead of where they are. They buy to reinforce their picture of where they're trying to get to. That, by the way, is why they outspend their incomes. There's a big, I own, therefore I am. I wear this particular shoe because that makes me a certain person. Whether anybody recognizes it or not, and the right people will recognize it. I'll bet you Susan can recognize an $800 shoe from half a mile away. So she would know. There's three types here of upscale differentiation. One is all about the symbolism, the brand, the designer name, what it means. I own, therefore I am. Another one is expertise. So affluent people want to buy not just the best in terms of luxury, but the best in terms of expertise. So that's why boundaries go away. The more affluent your customer, the less the styrofoam cup of geographic boundaries applies, and the more they will travel from distances to come to you because they're not buying by convenience. They don't care if their financial advisor is down the street or at the opposite end of the country. They want the best financial advisor they can find. They don't care if the dentist is down the street or at the opposite end of the country. They want the best dentist they can find. So the chop shop dentist, the fixum guy that people go to makes just broken teeth. Doc, just pull it. It's back here. Those dentists, real narrow geographic radius. Same day, $395 denture clinic, real narrow geographic radius. $50,000 cosmetic case, anywhere in the country. Anywhere in the country. Most of those practices, 20% of their patients come from 100 miles or more away. Some come from overseas. The geographic boundaries start to go away because they're all about buying expertise. Let me show you one expertise tool. Just give you a little takeaway. And this, by the way, you can find in the book, too. So here is, this is a piece out of Craig Proctor package. So this is real estate. This is residential, normal real estate. So what happens from listing to sale? And this now is the diagram of everything that happens once your house is listed. Now to be fair, probably half of the things in the diagram everybody does. At least a third of them have to be done. Some of them are just titled different. For example, putting the thing in the multiple listing service. Everybody does that. I think actually you probably have to do it. But nobody puts it together in a diagram. Nobody shows this whole system of what we do. So you immediately take a hodgepodge of things, tasks to do lists, things that you do, and you use it to demonstrate expertise. Third big thing everybody wants is exclusivity. Higher up the affluence ladder you go, the more they want exclusivity. In high-rise condos there's never a problem selling the penthouse. Never, never, never, never, never. Penthouses are always sold. Lower floors, not necessarily so easy. Penthouse? Gone. Why? Because there's only one of them. It's that simple. There's 70 floors, but there's only one penthouse. So you want to create penthouses. Gee, if you could only create 70 penthouses, that would pretty much solve the high-rise condo selling problem. You can't, although some of them claim three penthouses. They're trying that. There's the penthouse, penthouse, and penthouse floor. Well, how can it be a penthouse if it's three floors down? Because we called it a penthouse, that's why. So they're trying. So exclusivity, that's the third big trigger. They want what not everybody else has. See, I could add a zero, as goofy as some of those prices are. I could add a zero and get it if I was willing to say there'd only be 100 of them. Or we'll only sell that sweater to one person in your town, so you will never walk into a room and see somebody else wearing it. I could add a zero. Did I change the value of the sweater? No, same sweater. I just added exclusivity. Very powerful marketing tool, especially with affluent people. So you want to figure out what is there about your product, your service, or your business? How can it be positioned? How can it be presented so that it can be sold at premium prices to people who do not make their decisions based upon price and have a urgent need or passionate level of desire in the category in which you operate?
